papers.title: On a problem of E. Meckes for the unitary eigenvalue process on an arc
papers.abstract: We study the problem originally communicated by E. Meckes on the asymptotics for the eigenvalues of the kernel of the unitary eigenvalue process of a random n×n\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$n \times n$$\end{document} matrix. The eigenvalues pj\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$p_{j}$$\end{document} of the kernel are, in turn, associated with the discrete prolate spheroidal wave functions. We consider the eigenvalue counting function |G(x,n)|:=#{j:pj>Ce-xn}\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$|G(x,n)|:=\#\{j:p_j>Ce^{-x n}\}$$\end{document}, (C>0\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$C>0$$\end{document} here is a fixed constant) and establish the asymptotic behavior of its average over the interval x∈(λ-ε,λ+ε)\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$x \in (\lambda -\varepsilon , \lambda +\varepsilon )$$\end{document} by relating the function |G(x, n)| to the solution J(q) of the following energy problem on the unit circle S1\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$S^{1}$$\end{document}, which is of independent interest. Namely, for given θ\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\theta $$\end{document}, 0<θ<2π\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$0<\theta < 2 \pi $$\end{document}, and given q, 0<q<1\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$0<q<1$$\end{document}, we determine the function J(q)=inf{I(μ):μ∈P(S1),μ(Aθ)=q}\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$J(q) =\inf \{I(\mu ): \mu \in \mathcal {P}(S^{1}), \mu (A_{\theta }) = q\}$$\end{document}, where I(μ):=∫∫log1|z-ζ|dμ(z)dμ(ζ)\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$I(\mu ):= \int \!\int \log \frac{1}{|z - \zeta |} d\mu (z) d\mu (\zeta )$$\end{document} is the logarithmic energy of a probability measure μ\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mu $$\end{document} supported on the unit circle and Aθ\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$A_{\theta }$$\end{document} is the arc from e-iθ/2\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$e^{-i \theta /2}$$\end{document} to eiθ/2\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$e^{i \theta /2}$$\end{document}.

papers.title: Open problems on polynomials, their zero distribution and related questions: 2023
papers.abstract: This paper collects open problems that were presented at the ``Hausdorff Geometry of Polynomials"workshop held on July 10-14, 2023 in Sofia, Bulgaria.

papers.title: Almost optimal polynomial approximation on convex sets in C\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mathbb {C}
papers.abstract: For a function f, continuous on a compact convex set K, and analytic in its interior, we construct a sequence of almost optimal polynomials that converge with a geometric rate at points of analyticity of f. We also give an example of a non-convex K that does not have analytic boundary, but nevertheless permits geometric convergence of almost optimal approximants to the function f.

papers.title: Polynomial Approximation of Piecewise Analytic Functions on Quasi-Smooth Arcs
papers.abstract: For a function f that is piecewise analytic on a quasi-smooth arc L\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mathcal {L}$$\end{document} and any 0<σ<1\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$0<\sigma <1$$\end{document} we construct a sequence of polynomials that converge at a rate e-nσ\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$e^{-n^{\sigma }}$$\end{document} at each point of analyticity of f and are close to the best polynomial approximants on the whole L\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mathcal {L}$$\end{document}. Moreover, we give examples when such polynomials can be constructed for σ=1\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\sigma =1$$\end{document}.

papers.title: Weighted Minkowski's Existence Theorem and Projection Bodies
papers.abstract:
The Brunn-Minkowski Theory has seen several generalizations over the past century. Many of the core ideas have been generalized to measures. With the goal of framing these generalizations as a weighted Brunn-Minkowski theory, we prove the Minkowski existence theorem for a large class of Borel measures with continuous density, denoted by $\Lambda^n$: for $
u$ a finite, even Borel measure on the unit sphere and even $\mu\in\Lambda^n$, there exists a symmetric convex body $K$ such that $$d
u(u)=c_{\mu,K}dS^{\mu}_{K}(u),$$ where $c_{\mu,K}$ is a quantity that depends on $\mu$ and $K$ and $dS^{\mu}_{K}(u)$ is the surface area-measure of $K$ with respect to $\mu$. Examples of measures in $\Lambda^n$ are homogeneous measures (with $c_{\mu,K}=1$) and probability measures with radially decreasing densities (e.g. the Gaussian measure). We will also consider weighted projection bodies $\Pi_\mu K$ by classifying them and studying the isomorphic Shephard problem: if $\mu$ and $
u$ are even, homogeneous measures with density and $K$ and $L$ are symmetric convex bodies such that $\Pi_{\mu} K \subset \Pi_{
u} L$, then can one find an optimal quantity $\mathcal{A}>0$ such that $\mu(K)\leq \mathcal{A}
u(L)$? Among other things, we show that, in the case where $\mu=
u$ and $L$ is a projection body, $\mathcal{A}=1$.

papers.title: "Near-best" polynomial approximation of harmonic functions on compact sets in ℂ
